lecture_title: DBMS Syllabus for IBPS SO IT Officer 2025
title: DBMS Overview & Exam Weightage for IBPS SO IT Officer
explanation: DBMS (Database Management System) is the single most high-weightage topic for the IBPS SO IT Officer Mains exam. Out of the total 60 marks in the professional knowledge paper, DBMS alone accounts for approximately 15–20 marks — which is nearly equal to or greater than the typical mains cutoff of 13–14 marks. This means mastering DBMS alone can help a candidate qualify the mains exam. The syllabus must be covered with breadth (width) rather than extreme depth — every topic should be known at a moderate level.

concepts.definition: The professional knowledge exam for IT Officer recruitment under IBPS Specialist Officer, consisting of 60 marks of technical questions covering topics like DBMS, Networking, OS, Software Engineering, etc.
DBMS carries approximately 15–20 marks out of 60 in the IT Officer Mains paper — the highest weightage among all subjects.

examples.sentence: The mains cutoff for IT Officer is around 13–14 marks, and DBMS alone gives you 15–20 marks — so DBMS alone is sufficient to cross the cutoff.
examples.rule: DBMS is the single most important topic for IT Officer Mains.
examples.working: Cutoff ≈ 13–14 marks. DBMS weightage ≈ 15–20 marks out of 60. Therefore: DBMS marks > cutoff, making it the most strategic subject to master.

visual_moments.description: DBMS syllabus intro slide for IBPS SO IT Officer
DBMS exam weightage — 15-20 marks out of 60

teacher_quotes: Terah-chaudah number ka toh akela DBMS hi aapko mil jayega exam mein. DBMS iklota hi kaafi hai IT officer mains ko qualify karne mein.
Hume width mein jaana hai. Hume chaudai. Aapko har topic ki knowledge honi chahiye. Zyada deep mein jaane ki zaroorat nahi hai but at least moderate level ka pata hona chahiye.
traps: Do NOT skip any topic — the teacher has written actual exam questions, not just topic names.
DBMS alone can qualify you, but don't ONLY study DBMS — cover other subjects too for safety margin.
tricks: Strategy: Cover all DBMS topics at moderate depth (width-first) rather than going deep into just one or two topics.

title: Three Schema Architecture & Relational Model Basics
explanation: The Three Schema Architecture (also called ANSI/SPARC architecture) divides a database system into three levels: External (View) Level, Conceptual (Logical) Level, and Internal (Physical) Level. Direct questions are asked about each level — what is internal schema, what is view level, etc. The Relational Model is the foundation of modern RDBMS, where data is organized in tables (relations). Key terminology includes: Table/Relation (the 2D structure), Tuple/Row (a single record), Attribute/Column (a property), Arity (number of columns), Cardinality (number of rows), and Degree.

concepts.definition: ANSI/SPARC architecture with three levels: External (user views), Conceptual (logical structure), and Internal (physical storage). Direct exam questions are asked on each level.
Describes the physical storage structure of the database — how data is actually stored on disk (files, indices, storage allocation).
The highest level of abstraction — describes only a part of the database relevant to a particular user or application.
A data model where data is organized in tables (relations) with rows (tuples) and columns (attributes). The basis of all RDBMS.
The number of columns (attributes) in a table/relation. Also called the degree of the relation.
The number of rows (tuples) in a table/relation. Cardinality changes as records are inserted/deleted.
A single row in a relational table, representing one record or instance of an entity.

examples.sentence: If a Student table has 5 columns (Roll No, Name, Age, Class, Marks) and 100 rows, then Arity = 5 and Cardinality = 100.
examples.rule: Arity = number of columns; Cardinality = number of rows.
examples.working: Columns: Roll No, Name, Age, Class, Marks → Arity = 5. Rows: 100 student records → Cardinality = 100.

exercise_questions: What is the arity of a table with 7 attributes?
Differentiate between internal schema and external schema.
What is a tuple in a relational database?

visual_moments.description: Three Schema Architecture slide
Relational Model — table, arity, cardinality, tuples

teacher_quotes: Three schema architecture, jiski upar direct aapko question mil jaata hai. Internal schema, view level ke upar question aapko direct aa jayega.
traps: Arity = columns (NOT rows). Cardinality = rows (NOT columns). This is a very common confusion in MCQs.
Degree and Arity mean the same thing — number of attributes/columns.
tricks: Mnemonic: 'Arity' has an 'A' for 'Attributes' (columns). 'Cardinality' has a 'C' for 'Count of rows'.

title: Relational Algebra: Operations, Joins & Set Operations
explanation: Relational Algebra is a procedural query language that uses operators and symbols to manipulate relations (tables). It includes unary operations (Selection σ, Projection π) and binary operations (Union ∪, Intersection ∩, Set Difference −, Cartesian Product ×). Joins are critical for combining data from multiple tables: Inner Join, Outer Join (Left/Right/Full), Cross Join, and Natural Join. Questions can be symbol-based (given a relational algebra expression, determine the output) or conceptual (what does Selection vs Projection do).

concepts.definition: A procedural query language that uses special operators (σ, π, ⋈, ∪, ∩, −) to retrieve and manipulate data from relational databases.
A unary operation that selects rows (tuples) from a relation that satisfy a given predicate/condition. Operates horizontally.
A unary operation that selects specific columns (attributes) from a relation, removing duplicates. Operates vertically.
Operations that combine rows from two or more tables based on a related column. Types: Inner Join, Outer Join (Left/Right/Full), Cross Join, Natural Join.

examples.sentence: Selection (σ) selects ROWS: σ(Age > 25)(Student) returns all students older than 25. Projection (π) selects COLUMNS: π(Name, Age)(Student) returns only Name and Age columns.
examples.rule: Selection = horizontal (filters rows by condition). Projection = vertical (picks specific columns).
examples.working: σ operates on rows → WHERE clause equivalent. π operates on columns → SELECT clause equivalent.

exercise_questions: What is the difference between Selection and Projection in relational algebra?
Explain Natural Join vs Cross Join with an example.
What does the Union operation require from two relations?

visual_moments.description: Relational Algebra — basic symbol-based operations
Joins: inner, outer, cross, natural + Union, Intersection, Minus

teacher_quotes: Relational algebra mein aapke basic jo hai woh symbol based question aa sakte hain. Projection kya hota hai? Selection kya hota hai?
Joins chaahe ismein baat kar lo, chaahe SQL mein, baat ek hi hai.
traps: Selection selects ROWS (horizontal), NOT columns. Projection selects COLUMNS (vertical), NOT rows. Don't confuse them!
Union requires both relations to have the same number of attributes (union-compatible). This is a common MCQ trap.
Cross Join = Cartesian Product = every row of Table A combined with every row of Table B.
tricks: Remember: σ (sigma) = Selection = rows. π (pi) = Projection = columns.
Natural Join automatically matches on common column names — no explicit condition needed.

title: ER Model, Relationships & Types of Keys
explanation: The Entity-Relationship (ER) Model is a conceptual data model using ER Diagrams to represent entities, attributes (simple, composite, multivalued, derived), and relationships (1:1, 1:M, M:N). Keys are fundamental to relational databases: Primary Key (unique identifier), Foreign Key (references another table's primary key), Candidate Key (minimal superkey), Super Key (uniquely identifies rows), and Alternate Key (unused candidate keys). ER modeling is a core design step.

concepts.definition: Entity-Relationship Model — a conceptual framework for database design using entities, attributes, and relationships, represented through ER diagrams.
A column (or set of columns) that uniquely identifies each row in a table. Cannot contain NULL values. Each table has exactly one primary key.
A column in one table that references the primary key of another table, establishing a relationship between the two tables.
A minimal set of attributes that can uniquely identify a tuple. A table can have multiple candidate keys; one is chosen as the primary key.
Any set of one or more attributes that, taken collectively, can uniquely identify a row. Every candidate key is a super key, but not vice versa.
Candidate keys that are NOT selected as the primary key. They can still uniquely identify rows but are not the primary identifier.

examples.sentence: In a Student table with columns (Roll_No, Aadhar_No, Name, Age): Roll_No and Aadhar_No are both candidate keys. If Roll_No is chosen as primary key, then Aadhar_No becomes the alternate key.
examples.rule: Primary Key ⊂ Candidate Keys ⊂ Super Keys. Alternate Key = Candidate Key − Primary Key.
examples.working: Candidate Keys: {Roll_No}, {Aadhar_No} (both can uniquely identify a student). Primary Key chosen: Roll_No. Alternate Key: Aadhar_No. Super Keys: {Roll_No}, {Aadhar_No}, {Roll_No, Name}, {Roll_No, Age}, etc.

exercise_questions: What is the difference between a candidate key and a super key?
If a table has 3 candidate keys, how many alternate keys does it have if one is chosen as primary key?
Explain one-to-many relationship with an example.

visual_moments.description: ER Model — diagrams and attribute types
Keys: Primary, Foreign, Candidate, Super, Alternate

teacher_quotes: Primary key kya hai? Foreign key kya hai? Candidate key kya hai? Super key kya hai? Alternate key kya hai? Ye saare points aapko pata hone chahiye.
traps: Every Primary Key is a Candidate Key, and every Candidate Key is a Super Key — but NOT vice versa.
A table can have MULTIPLE candidate keys but only ONE primary key.
Foreign Key CAN contain NULL values (unlike primary key).
tricks: Key hierarchy: Super Key ⊃ Candidate Key ⊃ Primary Key. Alternate Key = Candidate Key − Primary Key.
Answer to 'How many alternate keys?' = (Number of candidate keys) − 1.

title: Functional Dependencies, Closure & Normalization (1NF–BCNF)
explanation: Functional Dependencies (FDs) state that one attribute determines another (X → Y). Attribute Closure (X⁺) finds all attributes determined by X, which is used to identify candidate keys. Normalization reduces redundancy: 1NF eliminates repeating groups, 2NF removes partial dependencies, 3NF removes transitive dependencies, and BCNF ensures every determinant is a candidate key. Numericals on closure and candidate keys are high-yield exam topics.

concepts.definition: A relationship X → Y where each value of attribute X uniquely determines the value of attribute Y. Example: Roll_No → Name.
The set of all attributes functionally determined by attribute set X using the given FDs. Used to find candidate keys.
The process of organizing database tables to minimize redundancy and dependency. Normal forms: 1NF → 2NF → 3NF → BCNF → 4NF → 5NF.
A table is in 1NF if it contains only atomic (indivisible) values — no repeating groups or multi-valued attributes.
A table is in 2NF if it is in 1NF AND has no partial dependency (no non-prime attribute depends on a part of a candidate key).
A table is in 3NF if it is in 2NF AND has no transitive dependency (no non-prime attribute depends on another non-prime attribute).
A stricter version of 3NF. A table is in BCNF if for every functional dependency X → Y, X is a superkey.

examples.sentence: Given FDs: {A→B, B→C, A→D}. Find A⁺ (closure of A). Start with A⁺ = {A}. A→B adds B → {A,B}. A→D adds D → {A,B,D}. B→C adds C → {A,B,C,D}. So A⁺ = {A,B,C,D} = all attributes. Therefore A is a candidate key.
examples.rule: Start with the attribute set, repeatedly apply FDs until no new attributes can be added. If closure = all attributes, it's a candidate key.
examples.working: A⁺: Start {A} → Apply A→B → {A,B} → Apply A→D → {A,B,D} → Apply B→C → {A,B,C,D}. Closure contains all attributes, so A is a candidate key.

exercise_questions: Given FDs: {A→BC, C→D, D→A}, find all candidate keys.
What is the difference between 2NF and 3NF?
Is every 3NF table also in BCNF? Explain with an example.

visual_moments.description: Functional Dependencies topic
Closure properties — finding candidate keys from FDs
Normalization: 2NF, 3NF, BCNF, 4NF, 5NF

teacher_quotes: Table diya hua hai. Ek attribute ka closure find out karo aur batao candidate key kaun si hogi? Kitni candidate keys hongi? Woh wala topic important hai.
First se lekar second, third, BCNF, fourth and fifth saari ki saari hi karni hai.
traps: Every BCNF table is in 3NF, but NOT every 3NF table is in BCNF. BCNF is stricter.
Partial dependency (violates 2NF) occurs only when the primary key is composite — if the PK is a single attribute, 2NF is automatically satisfied.
Closure is used to FIND candidate keys — it's not the same as a candidate key itself.
tricks: Normal form order: 1NF → 2NF (no partial dep) → 3NF (no transitive dep) → BCNF (every determinant is a superkey).
To find candidate keys: compute closure of each attribute/combination. If closure = all attributes, it's a candidate key.

title: SQL: Commands (DDL/DML/DCL/TCL), Clauses & Functions
explanation: SQL is categorized into DDL (CREATE, ALTER, DROP, TRUNCATE), DML (SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE), DCL (GRANT, REVOKE), and TCL (COMMIT, ROLLBACK, SAVEPOINT). Crucial clauses include ORDER BY, GROUP BY (with HAVING), and nested subqueries. Functions are split into Aggregate Functions (COUNT, SUM, AVG, MAX, MIN) operating on row groups, and Single Row Functions (UPPER, LENGTH) operating on individual rows.

concepts.definition: Commands that define/modify the database schema: CREATE (create table), ALTER (modify structure), DROP (delete table), TRUNCATE (delete all rows but keep structure).
Commands that manipulate data within tables: SELECT (retrieve), INSERT (add), UPDATE (modify), DELETE (remove rows).
Commands that control access permissions: GRANT (give privileges), REVOKE (take back privileges).
Commands for transaction management: COMMIT (save), ROLLBACK (undo), SAVEPOINT (set restore point).
Functions that operate on a group of rows and return a single value: COUNT, SUM, AVG, MAX, MIN. Used with GROUP BY.
Functions that operate on individual rows and return one result per row: UPPER, LOWER, LENGTH, SUBSTR, ROUND, etc.

examples.sentence: DDL: CREATE TABLE Student (id INT, name VARCHAR(50)); DML: INSERT INTO Student VALUES (1, 'Rahul'); DCL: GRANT SELECT ON Student TO user1; TCL: COMMIT;
examples.rule: DDL = Schema operations. DML = Data operations. DCL = Permission operations. TCL = Transaction operations.
examples.working: CREATE/ALTER/DROP/TRUNCATE → DDL (defines structure). SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE → DML (manipulates data). GRANT/REVOKE → DCL (controls access). COMMIT/ROLLBACK/SAVEPOINT → TCL (manages transactions).

exercise_questions: Classify these SQL commands into DDL/DML/DCL/TCL: CREATE, INSERT, GRANT, ROLLBACK, ALTER, DELETE, REVOKE, COMMIT.
What is the difference between DROP and TRUNCATE?
What is the difference between WHERE and HAVING clause?

visual_moments.description: SQL commands — DDL, DML, DCL, TCL
SQL clauses — ORDER BY, GROUP BY, subqueries
Functions — aggregate and single row functions

teacher_quotes: SQL mein har baar question aana hi aana hai DDL commands ke upar, DML commands ke upar, DCL commands ke upar.
ORDER BY kya hota hai? GROUP BY kya hota hai? Aggregate functions kya hain? Ye bhi basic basic pata hona chahiye.
traps: DROP deletes the entire table (structure + data). TRUNCATE deletes all data but keeps the table structure. DELETE removes specific rows.
TRUNCATE is DDL (not DML) — it cannot be rolled back in most RDBMS.
WHERE filters BEFORE grouping. HAVING filters AFTER grouping (used with GROUP BY).
SELECT is a DML command, even though it doesn't modify data.
tricks: Mnemonic for DDL: 'CATS' — Create, Alter, Truncate, (Drop belongs too but CATD sounds weird).
Mnemonic for DML: 'SIDU' — Select, Insert, Delete, Update.
Aggregate functions: 'CSMMA' — Count, Sum, Max, Min, Avg.

title: Transactions: ACID Properties, Concurrency Control & Indexing
explanation: Transactions follow ACID: Atomicity (all-or-none), Consistency, Isolation (concurrency control via shared/exclusive locks), and Durability. Concurrency control prevents anomalies like dirty reads or lost updates. Indexing speeds up queries and includes Primary Index (on key), Cluster Index (on non-key ordered field), and Secondary Index (on unordered field). B/B+ trees are the primary index structures.

concepts.definition: Atomicity (all-or-nothing), Consistency (valid state transitions), Isolation (concurrent independence), Durability (permanent after commit).
Mechanisms to manage simultaneous access to the database by multiple transactions, preventing conflicts using locks, timestamps, or validation.
Shared Lock (S-lock): allows multiple readers. Exclusive Lock (X-lock): allows only one writer. Used for concurrency control.
An index on the primary key of an ordered file. The file is physically sorted on the primary key.
Balanced tree data structures used for database indexing. B+ Tree stores data only in leaf nodes and links leaves for range queries — most commonly used in RDBMS.

examples.sentence: Bank transfer: Transfer ₹500 from Account A to Account B. Atomicity ensures either both debit and credit happen, or neither happens.
examples.rule: Atomicity = all operations in a transaction succeed together, or all fail together.
examples.working: Step 1: Debit ₹500 from A. Step 2: Credit ₹500 to B. If Step 2 fails, Step 1 is rolled back. The database never reflects a partial transfer.

exercise_questions: Explain each ACID property with a real-world example.
What is the difference between a shared lock and an exclusive lock?
Differentiate between B-Tree and B+ Tree.

visual_moments.description: ACID properties and transaction/concurrency control
Indexing: primary, cluster, secondary index, B/B+ tree structure

teacher_quotes: ACID properties kya hain? Basic transaction concurrency control ka question aa sakta hai. Types of locks ke upar basic level ka.
B and B-tree ka structure, basic structure jo hai, zyada detail mein nahi basic structure aapko pata hona chahiye.
traps: B-Tree stores data in ALL nodes (internal + leaf). B+ Tree stores data ONLY in leaf nodes — leaves are linked for efficient range queries.
Shared Lock allows concurrent reads but blocks writes. Exclusive Lock blocks both reads and writes by others.
Durability means AFTER commit — uncommitted changes are NOT durable and can be rolled back.
tricks: ACID mnemonic: 'A-C-I-D' = All-or-nothing, Consistent state, Independent transactions, Data survives crashes.
B+ Tree is preferred over B-Tree in RDBMS because leaf linking enables fast range queries.

title: Views & PL/SQL: Triggers, Cursors, Stored Procedures
explanation: Views are virtual tables defined by SELECT queries to customize data access. Views are highly tested. PL/SQL adds procedural flow to SQL: Triggers execute on events (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE), Cursors process query results row-by-row (Implicit/Explicit), and Stored Procedures are named, precompiled code blocks. Basic understanding of their definitions and purposes is sufficient for the exam.

concepts.definition: Virtual tables defined by SQL queries. They do not store data themselves — they dynamically reflect the underlying tables. Very frequently asked in exams.
PL/SQL procedures that automatically execute in response to specific database events (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE on a table).
Database pointers used to process query results row-by-row. Types: Implicit (automatic) and Explicit (programmer-defined).
Precompiled collections of SQL statements stored in the database, callable by name. Improve performance and code reusability.

examples.sentence: CREATE VIEW TopStudents AS SELECT Name, Marks FROM Student WHERE Marks > 90; — This creates a virtual table showing only high-scoring students.
examples.rule: A View is a saved SELECT query that acts like a virtual table. No physical data is stored.
examples.working: The View 'TopStudents' dynamically shows Name and Marks of students with Marks > 90. If underlying Student table changes, the View automatically reflects the change.

exercise_questions: What is a View? Can we update data through a View?
What is the difference between a Trigger and a Stored Procedure?
Name the two types of cursors in PL/SQL.

visual_moments.description: Views — very important topic, frequently asked
PL/SQL: Triggers, Cursors, Stored Procedures

teacher_quotes: Views, very very important. Views ke upar question bahut baar aata hai.
Triggers kya hai? Kyon use karte hain? Cursors kya hain? Stored procedure kya hai? Kyon use karte hain? Zyada detail mein nahi.
traps: Views do NOT store data physically — they are virtual tables derived from queries.
Not all Views are updatable — Views with JOINs, GROUP BY, DISTINCT, or aggregates are generally NOT updatable.
Triggers fire AUTOMATICALLY; Stored Procedures must be CALLED explicitly.
tricks: View = Virtual table = Saved query. Trigger = Automatic procedure. Stored Procedure = Manual call.
Cursors are needed when you want to process results ONE ROW AT A TIME instead of as a set.

title: RAID Levels: Redundant Array of Independent Disks
explanation: RAID (Redundant Array of Independent/Inexpensive Disks) is a data storage technology that combines multiple physical disks into a single logical unit for redundancy, performance, or both. RAID has multiple levels (0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 10) each with different configurations of striping, mirroring, and parity. This topic is very important — direct questions appear 'every year' in the IT Officer exam. Candidates should know the basic characteristics and trade-offs of each RAID level.

concepts.definition: Redundant Array of Independent (or Inexpensive) Disks — a technology that uses multiple disks together for redundancy, improved performance, or both.
Data is split across disks for speed. No redundancy — if one disk fails, all data is lost. Best performance, zero fault tolerance.
Data is duplicated on two disks. Full redundancy — if one disk fails, the mirror has a complete copy. 50% storage efficiency.
Data and parity information are striped across 3+ disks. Can tolerate one disk failure. Most commonly used RAID level.

examples.sentence: RAID 0: 4 disks of 100GB each → 400GB usable (100% efficiency, 0% redundancy). RAID 1: 4 disks of 100GB → 200GB usable (50% efficiency, full mirroring).
examples.rule: RAID 0 = maximum storage + speed, zero protection. RAID 1 = 50% storage, full protection.
examples.working: RAID 0: All 4×100GB = 400GB available. One disk fails → all data lost. RAID 1: 4 disks paired into 2 mirrors → 2×100GB = 200GB usable.

exercise_questions: Compare RAID 0, RAID 1, and RAID 5 in terms of redundancy, performance, and storage efficiency.
How many disk failures can RAID 5 tolerate?
What is the minimum number of disks required for RAID 5?

visual_moments.description: RAID levels — yearly exam question

teacher_quotes: Har saal iske upar question aata tha. RAID levels direct question har baar aapke saamne aata hai.
traps: RAID 0 has NO redundancy — it is NOT fault-tolerant. Don't assume all RAID levels provide redundancy.
RAID 5 needs MINIMUM 3 disks and can tolerate only ONE disk failure.
RAID is NOT a backup solution — it protects against hardware failure, not data corruption or deletion.
tricks: RAID 0 = Speed only (0 redundancy). RAID 1 = Mirror (1 copy). RAID 5 = Parity (popular in enterprise).
Quick storage calculation: RAID 1 = 50% of total. RAID 5 = (N−1)/N of total (where N = number of disks).

title: NoSQL, Big Data, BASE & CAP Theorem
explanation: NoSQL databases handle unstructured data scale and are categorized into Key-Value, Document, Column-family, and Graph. Big Data is defined by the 5 V's (Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value), processed using Hadoop (HDFS, MapReduce). BASE properties focus on soft state and eventual consistency. CAP Theorem states a distributed system can only guarantee two of Consistency, Availability, and Partition Tolerance.

concepts.definition: Non-relational databases designed for large-scale, flexible data. Types: Key-Value, Document, Column-family, Graph.
3V's: Volume (size), Velocity (speed), Variety (types). 5V's add: Veracity (quality/trust) and Value (usefulness).
Basically Available (always responds), Soft state (state may change over time), Eventually consistent (will become consistent eventually). NoSQL counterpart of ACID.
In a distributed system, you can guarantee only 2 of 3: Consistency (all nodes see same data), Availability (system always responds), Partition tolerance (works despite network splits).
A framework for distributed storage (HDFS) and processing (MapReduce) of Big Data. Tools include Hive, Pig, HBase, Spark, etc.

examples.sentence: NoSQL Types: Key-Value (Redis — caching), Document (MongoDB — JSON-like docs), Column-family (Cassandra — wide columns), Graph (Neo4j — social networks).
examples.rule: Four types of NoSQL databases, each suited for different use cases.
examples.working: Key-Value → simple lookup (shopping cart). Document → flexible schemas (content management). Column-family → massive write throughput (IoT). Graph → connected data (social networks, recommendations).

exercise_questions: Name the 5 V's of Big Data.
What are the four types of NoSQL databases? Give one example each.
Compare ACID and BASE properties.
State the CAP Theorem. Why can't all three be satisfied simultaneously?

visual_moments.description: NoSQL and Big Data — 3V's/5V's, Hadoop
BASE and CAP properties
Summary — 15 marks on average from DBMS

teacher_quotes: Three V's ya five V's of Big Data ya fir Hadoop ka ecosystem ke andar kya-kya tools hain? Woh bhi bilkul basic level ke.
Jaise ACID properties hain, aisi BASE properties hoti hain, CAP properties hain. Sirf basic basic note karke jaana.
traps: BASE is NOT the opposite of ACID — BASE is an alternative consistency model for distributed NoSQL systems.
CAP Theorem: you MUST sacrifice one of the three (C, A, or P). Most distributed systems sacrifice Consistency (choose AP).
NoSQL does NOT mean 'No SQL' — it means 'Not Only SQL'. Some NoSQL databases support SQL-like queries.
tricks: ACID = strong consistency (RDBMS). BASE = eventual consistency (NoSQL).
5 V's mnemonic: Very Very Various, Very Valuable (Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value).
CAP: Most cloud databases (Cassandra, DynamoDB) choose AP (availability + partition tolerance) over strong consistency.
